intro:
Arabia is the vast peninsula southeast of Palestine, bounded by the Red Sea on the west, the Persian Gulf on the east, and the Syrian and Mesopotamian deserts to the north. In Scripture it is largely the land of desert-dwelling nomads — Ishmaelites, Midianites, Kedarites, and other tribes descended from Abraham’s sons by Hagar and Keturah (Genesis 25:1-6; Genesis 25:12-16) — and the source of caravan wealth in gold, spices, and precious stones. The peoples of Joktan and Cush in the Table of Nations are associated with its regions (Genesis 10:7).

Arabia’s deserts form the backdrop of much of Israel’s early story. It was in the Arabian wilderness of Midian that Moses kept Jethro’s flocks and met God at the burning bush, and there at Sinai that Israel received the law (Exodus 3:1; Galatians 4:25). Arabian and Sabean traders brought their goods to Israel; the queen of Sheba came from the south with camels bearing spices and gold (1 Kings 10:1-2), and Solomon received yearly tribute from “all the kings of Arabia” (2 Chronicles 9:14). The prophets included Arabia’s tribes — Kedar, Dedan, Tema — in their oracles concerning the nations (Isaiah 21:13-17; Jeremiah 49:28-33).

In the New Testament Arabia is most notable in Paul’s testimony. After his conversion near Damascus he did not immediately consult others but “went away into Arabia, and returned again to Damascus” (Galatians 1:17) — a period, likely in the Nabataean kingdom, that prepared him for his apostolic mission. It was the Nabataean king Aretas’s governor who later guarded Damascus to seize him, prompting his escape over the wall (2 Corinthians 11:32-33).

At Pentecost, Arabians were among the God-fearing pilgrims who heard the apostles declare the mighty works of God in their own tongue (Acts 2:11), marking the gospel’s reach to the desert peoples on the edge of the biblical world. Arabia thus appears throughout Scripture as a land of nomads and traders, a place of divine encounter and preparation, and one more region gathered within the scope of God’s redemptive purposes.

link_tables.title: Places

cells: Petra
Rock-city capital of Nabataean Arabia, where Paul withdrew (Gal 1:17)
Sinai
Mountain in Arabia where Israel received the law (Gal 4:25)
Tema
North Arabian oasis named in the prophets' oracles (Isa 21:14)
Dedan
Caravan people of Arabia in the prophetic oracles (Isa 21:13)
Kedar
Nomadic Ishmaelite tribe of the Arabian desert (Isa 21:16)
Sheba
South Arabian land of spice-traders and its queen (1 Kgs 10:1)

link_tables.note: Click any name to open its article. Markers for these places appear on the map above.

cells: Paul
Withdrew into Arabia after his conversion (Gal 1:17)
Moses
Met God at the bush in the Arabian wilderness (Ex 3:1)
Hagar
Whose son's line peopled the Arabian deserts (Gen 25:12)
Ishmael
Father of twelve princes of the desert tribes (Gen 25:16)
Job
Righteous sufferer of the eastern desert land of Uz (Job 1:1)
Aretas
Nabataean king whose governor watched Damascus for Paul (2 Cor 11:32)
Geshem
The Arab who opposed the rebuilding of Jerusalem (Neh 2:19)
